relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Lazarus of Bethany is weak, bound to the household of Mary and Martha.
Mary’s future anointing is named before the grief unfolds.
The sisters send love-language: the one you love is weak.
The weakness is framed toward glory, not final death.
Jesus loves Martha, Mary, and Lazarus.
Love waits two days in mystery.
Then he turns toward Judea again.
The disciples remember stones and danger.
Daylight walking means seeing by the world’s light.
Night-walking stumbles without inner light.
Jesus names death as sleep to be awakened.
The disciples hear ordinary sleep and healing.
They misunderstand death-language.
Jesus says plainly: Lazarus died.
The delay becomes a field for trust.
Thomas speaks brave fatalism: let us die with him.
Four days in the tomb intensify finality.
Bethany sits close to Jerusalem.
Many gather to console the sisters.
Martha goes out to meet him while Mary remains seated.
Martha speaks grief and trust together.
Even now, she knows the Divine hears him.
Jesus promises her brother will rise.
Martha knows last-day resurrection.
Jesus reveals: I am resurrection and life.
Living trust participates in life beyond death.
Martha confesses Messiah, Son, and Coming One.
She calls Mary: the Teacher is here.
Mary rises quickly toward him.
Jesus remains outside the village where Martha met him.
The mourners follow Mary toward the tomb they assume she seeks.
Mary falls at his feet with the same grief.
Jesus is deeply moved by Mary’s weeping and communal grief.
Come and see leads him to the tomb.
Jesus weeps.
The people see love in his tears.
Others question why the eye-opener did not prevent death.
Deeply moved again, he comes to the stone-sealed cave.
Martha warns of fourth-day decay.
Trust is called toward seeing divine glory.
The stone is removed and Jesus thanks the Father.
The prayer is spoken for the crowd’s trust in the Sender.
The great voice calls Lazarus out.
The dead one comes out, and the community is told to loose him.
Many who came to Mary trust after seeing.
Others report the sign to the Pharisees.
The council fears the signs and their consequences.
They fear Roman loss of place and nation.
Caiaphas speaks as high priest in the crisis.
One-for-the-people logic is named.
His political reasoning becomes unintended prophecy.
The death will gather scattered children into one.
From that day, the death plot hardens.
Jesus withdraws near the wilderness and remains with disciples.
Passover nears and purification begins.
People seek him and wonder if he will come.
The authorities order information so they may seize him.
